ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for prawn curry; FOODON_03302051 (shrimp dish) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for prawn curry; A00LC (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for prawn curry as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of prawn curry is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of prawns in curry sauce, commonly served alongside steamed rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Crustaceans, shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (NDB 15150) for prawn base; coconut milk and spice contributions estimated from USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides a prawn curry composite entry. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for cooked shrimp (NDB 15150) and coconut milk (NDB 12117). Per-100g figures represent a typical prawn curry with coconut milk gravy at approximately 40% prawn by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for seafood curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with low-GI seafood and meat curry dishes (GI range 20–30) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The low GI reflects minimal starchy carbohydrate content; the dominant macronutrients are protein and fat from prawns and coconut milk. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (25 × 3.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 2.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size carefully, as a single 300 g serving contributes approximately 1260 mg sodium — over half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-salt preparation when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn curry has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.1 g per 100 g) and dietary cholesterol from prawns (~115 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from coconut milk and cholesterol from prawns warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, the omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein from prawns provide cardioprotective benefits when consumed in controlled portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn curry is a richly spiced seafood dish in which fresh or frozen prawns (shrimp) are simmered in a fragrant curry sauce typically made from coconut milk, tomatoes, onions, garlic, ginger, and a blend of ground spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli. The dish is widely prepared across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Singapore, where it appears on menus at Indian, Singaporean, and fusion restaurants. The prawns contribute a firm, succulent texture and a naturally sweet, briny flavour that contrasts with the warm heat of the curry spices. Coconut milk gives the sauce a creamy, slightly sweet body with a characteristic richness. Nutritionally, prawn curry is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns. Per 100 g it provides approximately 112 kcal, 10.2 g protein, 6.8 g fat (of which 4.1 g is saturated, largely from coconut milk), and only 3.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is a meaningful source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine from the prawns. Sodium content is elevated due to salt and spice pastes used in preparation, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 25 (low), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. Prawn curry is typically served with steamed rice, roti, or naan in restaurant settings.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (prawn curry composite) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for cooked shrimp and coconut milk. No single exact entry for this specific restaurant preparation exists; values represent a typical prawn curry with coconut milk gravy. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted source match.
notes: 1. The input record listed cuisine as 'Mexican' and cuisine_tags as Mexican/Taco/Burrito/Naan; however, the restaurants named ('Three Amigos Indian and Mexican Restaurant', 'Three Amigos Mexican and Indian - Boat Quay') serve Indian and Mexican food, and prawn curry is an Indian/Singaporean dish. Cuisine has been corrected to 'Indian / Singaporean'. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: prawn curry prepared from natural ingredients contains no industrially produced trans fats; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN data. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category; USDA SR Legacy does not report vitamin D for cooked shrimp in curry preparation. 4. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on seafood curry entries in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care / University of Sydney GI Database); no direct measurement for prawn curry exists in the approved GI database. 5. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish requirements (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy shrimp and coconut milk component data.
